Project Description:
The project began with the redesign of an underused site containing a church, a single-family house, and a parking lot located at the intersection of Victoria Drive and East 11th Avenue.

Working in teams of two, we developed a mini masterplan that divides the site into three parcels: the church and two new buildings. The proposal considers phased construction, with each parcel developed at different times, while exploring how built form can establish a hierarchy of spaces in response to the site’s social and physical context.

The project then transitioned into an individual phase, where each building design reflects and reinforces the core ideas established in the masterplan.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Physical model of the site with student's project on it
Image 1 Caption: The masterplan responds to the site’s shape and surrounding context. The west building, Elevated Blocks (by Angelica Chua), maintains a horizontal orientation, while the east building, Discovery, continues the angular geometry of the adjacent Immigrant Services Society of BC building (ISS) and the Millennium SkyTrain tracks. Positioned on the southern portion of the site, the west building creates open space between itself and the ISS building. This area serves as a shared open space for the ISS and establishes a grand entry sequence to the centrally located church. The east building acts as a sound barrier, buffering the site and surrounding neighborhood from train noise. Together, the three buildings define a semi-public open space along the southern edge of the site, designated for gardening and a playground. This quieter shared space is embraced by all three buildings, creating a buffer from the more public areas of the site.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Site Plan
Image 2 Caption: Inspired by the walkways and paths of Olympic Village, the plaza features a fountain and landscaped routes leading toward the church. On the southern portion of the site, a more semi-public area is designated for residents, including gardens and a playground.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Ground Floor Plan
Image 3 Caption: Discovery’s ground floor is designed to be resident-focused, with amenities that encourage connection and community. It provides spaces for neighbors to get to know one another, host small events, and gather with people from adjacent communities, including the ISS, the church, and nearby buildings. A guest unit is also included for residents to accommodate visiting friends and family.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Typical floor plan from level 2 to 6
Image 4 Caption: Discovery consists of two single-exit towers with a mix of unit types on each floor, including one-bedroom, two-bedroom, and three-bedroom units. This variety encourages diverse interactions among residents on every level. The building includes five refugee units, which are not fixed to specific locations. Instead, residents can choose any available three-bedroom unit across different floors, allowing for greater flexibility and integration within the community. All units on each floor share a single hallway, promoting daily encounters and a sense of immediate neighborhood. This layout encourages residents to become familiar with one another, fostering comfort, connection, and opportunities for casual interaction.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Typical unit plans of 1 bed, 2 bed, and 3 bed units
Image 5 Caption: Each unit is designed with a clear separation between private bedrooms and shared living spaces, along with efficient layouts for kitchens and bathrooms. In some units, the entry is located near the bedrooms; however, priority is given to the living areas, which are oriented to the south to maximize natural light and provide access to balconies.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Rooftop plan
Image 6 Caption: Units do not include in-suite laundry. Instead, a communal laundry area is located on the rooftop, inspired by Nightingale 1 in Australia. The rooftop also features a shared garden and viewing area, allowing residents to relax or spend time under a canopy while waiting for their laundry. This setup encourages encounters beyond each floor, helping residents connect and become more aware of the broader community within the building.
Image 7 Alt/Description: Sections of the building
Image 8 Alt/Description: Elevations of the building
Image 8 Caption: Discovery’s balconies alternate on each floor, creating opportunities for visual connection and interaction between residents. The building façade features fibre cement panels with a dark wood–like texture, giving the building a warm, approachable character.
Image 9 Alt/Description: Rooftop view of the building
Image 9 Caption: Rooftop view of Discovery, illustrating a shared space that fosters social interaction while framing views of the surrounding context.
Image 10 Alt/Description: Facade and balcony detail of the building
Image 10 Caption: This façade detail model illustrates the balcony design used in Discovery. Perforated balcony panels are used to support the building’s core concept of connection. With balconies alternating between floors, the perforation provides a balance of privacy and openness, offering more screening than glass guardrails while still allowing light and views for residents using the space.

Project Description:
Rise Again is a seven-story residential building that explores a missing middle housing approach, with a focus on community, rest, and connection. The number seven is intentional. It draws inspiration from the idea of rest, particularly the concept in Christianity where the seventh day is a day of rest. This idea is translated into architecture as a building that supports both daily life and moments of pause.

At the ground level, the first floor is designed as a shared community space.
It includes a community hall and kitchen, which act as the social heart of the building and allow residents to gather, share meals, and build relationships.

Bike parking is also located on this level, promoting sustainable transportation and supporting everyday convenience.

The upper six floors are dedicated to residential units. Each floor contains eight units, creating a consistent and efficient layout.

The repeated mountain motif across the building represents the mountains that the building itself partially blocks.

The façade becomes a symbolic gesture, reflecting the presence of the mountains and maintaining a visual connection to the landscape.

This idea is reinforced at the rooftop level, where residents have access to shared outdoor space with direct views of the mountains. This creates a full experience, from symbolic representation on the façade to real physical connection at the top.

The building is situated between two existing structures, which can create a sense of density and enclosure. To respond to this condition, I chose a palette of white and yellow.

White helps reflect light and makes the building feel more open and less heavy within the tight urban context. 

Yellow introduces warmth and brightness, helping to bring light into the space and create a more uplifting and welcoming atmosphere.

Together, these colours soften the density of the surroundings and enhance the overall experience of the building.

Project Description: Our project counters Cadillac Fariview's proposal of the Unilever Precinct site. Instead of massive office towers adjacent to single family housing neighbourhoods, we instead propose moderate density which is contextually sensitive. We reject CF's proposal for white- collar office jobs and instead harken back to the site's industrial past by promoting blue-collar work with new trade school. Utilizing the existing Unilever Soap factory, we preserve the industrial character of the site by adaptively reusing the building. The old industrial quiality is celebrated in its raw state with minimal yet intentional interventions. The trade school acts as an anchor for the new East Harbour Transit Hub attracting workers and visitors from all over Toronto and Beyond.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Row Housing, Infill Housing, Agriculture and Gardening
Project Description:
This village explores housing as a framework for community, centered on an inward-facing mews that fosters everyday social interaction. Rather than treating circulation as a purely functional element, the design repositions access as a social spine, where front doors, elevated walkways, and shared outdoor spaces collectively encourage informal encounters and cultivate belonging.

The site strategy is anchored by the retention of an existing mature tree, preserving a natural gathering point and organizes the public realm. From this anchor, the proposal establishes a deliberate gradient of public, semi-public, and private spaces, that supports both collective activity and individual retreat. The central mews serves as the primary organizing element, framed by residential units whose primary entrances face inward to enhance visibility, safety, and community engagement.

The building massing responds sensitively to context and program. A curved residential form defines the site's interior while lifting at strategic moments to introduce natural light, permeability, and vertical connections. Exterior walkways and terraces extend circulation into shared space, enabling residents to engage with the mews beyond their unit thresholds.

A diverse mix of unit types (studios, one, two, three-bedroom units) is distributed throughout the building to accommodate varied household needs. Integrated refugee housing is dispersed across multiple levels and locations to promote inclusion within the broader village rather than isolation. Proximity to shared amenities reinforces accessibility and social integration.

Precedents such as Peter Barber Architects' Edgewood Mews and Barcelona's Born Market inform the spatial and landscape strategies, particularly the use of simple forms, topography, and pedestrian-focused sequences to support flexible social use. Ultimately, Village Mews is a resilient system for fostering connection, community resilience, and shared urban life.

Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
The Interstice reimagines a site currently occupied by a church, proposing a redevelopment that better serves both the congregation and the surrounding community. The project introduces shared spaces designed to support community use, in collaboration with the nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS of BC).

As one of three residential buildings within a broader master plan, two of which are developed by Kyon Gicole, The Interstice focuses on providing housing for those seeking refuge. The unit mix prioritizes two- and three-bedroom layouts to accommodate families and larger households.

The building employs a single-loaded corridor strategy, enabling cross ventilation for passive cooling within the units. This exterior corridor also creates a generous overhang, offering shade during the summer while allowing solar access in the winter. Beyond its environmental performance, the corridor functions as a shared social space, encouraging interaction and fostering a sense of community among residents.

Outdoor areas are shaped by the building footprints, responding directly to site conditions and user needs. A tiered master plan minimizes shadowing between structures while maintaining adequate space for housing and essential services.

The Interstice demonstrates how thoughtful redevelopment can give back to the community, creating opportunities for connection, support, and shared public life through the spaces it defines.

Project Title: Thresholds of Resilience: Sheltering the Vulnerable, Without Isolation
Image Description/Alt Text\n: A rendering of the courtyard
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Option 9, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
How do we house people who have lost their homes without cutting them off from the life of a community? This question sits at the heart of Thresholds of Resilience. Responding to the "ReZoned" challenge, the project transforms underused church land into a place where below-market housing and refugee housing are not "added on" but woven in.

The masterplan starts by strengthening the existing neighborhood. A carefully renovated church is joined by a new residential block. By removing private parking, the site becomes a pedestrian-first "Shared Ground" - not a plaza in name only, but a daily gathering space with a community kitchen where church members, ISSofBC staff, and all residents can cook, eat, and linger together. This is not an event space; it is an everyday place. At street level, this thick wall opens into an inhabited colonnade - a covered walkway where neighbors can pause, meet, or simply sit without feeling exposed to the street's noise. 

The courtyard designed for calm, not spectacle. Gentle water features mask city sounds. A canopy of pink cherry blossoms - chosen to honor the magnolia's quiet symbolism of resilience and home-offers shade and beauty. The courtyard is shared by all residents, safety here comes from mutual presence, not surveillance.

This thinking continues inside. Single-loaded corridors are facing the courtyard, act like elevated green walkway. These are places to nod hello, to see children playing below, to feel part of something. A simple structural grid allows for diverse 1-to-4-bedroom homes, with refugee housing distributed across all floors, indistinguishable from market units. Equity is not announced; it is built. Instead of exposed balconies, winter gardens are carved into the façade, private, sheltered, but still connected to the life of the building.

In the end, this project is about housing as a foundation for everyday community where people can finally rest and once again, be resilient and hopeful.

Project Description:
This project is part of the larger Cascade Residence development, organized as a linear housing bar that frames a continuous shared outdoor space. The design transforms a typical residential strip into a social spine, where circulation, landscape, and community life intersect.

Ground-level pathways and green spaces encourage interaction and movement, while the articulated massing breaks down the scale and creates rhythm along the length of the building. Units are arranged to maximize light, views, and connection to the shared space.

The project explores how linear living can support a more connected and community-driven residential experience.

Variations in height, material tone, and window placement articulate each segment while maintaining an overall cohesive identity. Alternating light and dark cladding emphasizes the modular nature of the design, while recessed and projecting volumes create depth and shadow across the façade. A central break in the massing suggests a key പ്രവേശ or shared circulation point, reinforcing the idea of a connected spine that links the development together.

A central feature—likely a plaza or focal installation—anchors the composition, while softer landscaping elements such as trees and shrubs create a more intimate, human-scaled environment. The layout balances movement and pause, encouraging both circulation and social interaction within a semi-enclosed green spine.

Project Description:
The Stack is designed as a spark for social reintegration for the
unhoused population of New Westminster. By prioritizing intentional
spatial adjacencies, the project facilitates a transition from recovery to
stability. The program is divided into two residential tiers: individuals
currently undergoing rehabilitation for homelessness and substance
use, and those who have successfully transitioned through similar
supportive frameworks. These two demographics converge within
the central tower—a dedicated social volume designed to foster
peer mentorship, mutual support, and a shared sense of community
through lived experience. The Stack punctures into the library below
and is the driving force for spatial organizations, intended for people
to gather at the center and create connections of their own.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Coach House is a counter-proposal to the current development at the former Toronto Coach Terminal. Informed by GIS-based site studies of demographics, green coverage, and tree canopy, the proposal reimagines what a tower-and-podium typology can mean in downtown Toronto. Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Community land trusts, Housing design, Housing theory, Pedagogy, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Towers, Mixed Use
Project Description:
The Coach House is a counter-proposal to the Kilmer-Tricon redevelopment of the former Toronto Coach Terminal at Bay and Elizabeth.
The site has been cleared before. In 1858 it was a dense residential neighbourhood; today, residential coverage has fallen to 5%, a 90% loss from peak. Tree canopy sits below 10%, green space under 5%, and the Bay Street Corridor holds just 24% of Toronto's average density of children. This is not where families live.
The project refuses that pattern. Grounded in GIS analysis of demographics, canopy, circulation, and family infrastructure, it argues that memory and affordability are not opposed to density; they are its precondition. Where Kilmer-Tricon proposes 873 studios and one-bedrooms above a paramedic hub and office podium, The Coach House proposes 394 family units above a full K-8 elementary school for 542 students and a licensed daycare for 83 children. Elizabeth Street is closed to traffic and reopened as a pedestrian boulevard linking both buildings to a new park.
Two volumes work within the site's heritage designation and 55-metre flight-path limit: a 15-storey building on Bay Street with the school across floors 1 to 4 and housing above, and a 43-storey tower on Elizabeth with daycare at grade, shared amenity on floors 3 and 4, and family units to floor 43. Units rotate 45 degrees off the building axis, a diamond geometry that gives every home dual aspect, cross ventilation, and two balconies. Sizes range from 70 m² to 145 m², with 60%+ affordable tenure.
The result: 45% fewer units than the existing proposal, but more residents, six times more school-age children, and an active public ground. The heritage terminal becomes a community event space. School and daycare anchor the base. Families live above. Public land, finally, returns public benefit.

Home Studio\n: Manufacturing Otherwise — University of Manitoba
Tags: Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Infill Housing, Mixed Use, Land Back, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
It begins with a fragment of birch bark, often left behind. As a material-led design, it rethinks how materials are sourced, how waste can be revalued, and how architecture can participate in both ecological and social systems. Embedded within and expressed through the walls, birch bark cork becomes a tactile architectural material rooted in care, memory, and cultural practice, while challenging conventional material chains. 

Situated in the Spence Neighbourhood of Winnipeg, Manitoba, and developed in partnership with the Spence Neighbourhood Association (SNA), the project is designed for those who already inhabit and rely on the neighbourhood, particularly the local Indigenous community. It supports individuals who arrive during the day seeking resources, and to those who stay, offering transitional housing. The project exists within a condition of tension between innovation and feasibility, care and constraint, and between what is possible and what is permitted. For SNA, the urgency is practical, where spaces must welcome and endure. For architecture, the responsibility is broader, to move beyond extractive practices and toward accountability and care. 

The design is rooted in an intention to place Indigenous values at the centre of architectural making, and to position design as a medium for restoring connection and overcoming alienation. Birchstead Living operates as a negotiation between systems and stories, structure and softness, efficiency and care. Here, living is held not only within walls. It proposes an architecture where material, people, and place are continuous and never left behind. Re-grounding architecture as a practice of care and connection that holds memory while making space for what comes next.

Project Description:
This project combines a public library with supportive women’s housing to create a place of safety, learning, and belonging. The proposal responds to the need for stable housing that supports women and families experiencing housing insecurity while also providing access to community resources, education, and social connection.

The building is organized with a public library at the lower levels and residential housing above, maintaining clear separation between public and private spaces. Housing is arranged around a protected internal courtyard with shared gardens, play areas, and informal gathering spaces. Units transition through semi-private thresholds such as balconies, pocket spaces, and exterior corridors that support autonomy and choice.

Guided by trauma-informed design principles, the project emphasizes safety, clear sightlines, layered privacy, and welcoming communal spaces. Prospect and refuge inform both library and housing areas, balancing openness with shelter. Natural materials, daylight, ventilation, and adaptable screens create a warm, non-institutional environment that supports healing, dignity, and long-term stability.

Project Title: Reproducing the Suburb: Housing, Childcare Infrastructure, and the Gendered Burdens of Caregiving in Toronto’s Densifying Peripheries
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Black-and-white photograph of a suburban backyard in 1943 Canada, showing a mother saying goodbye to her two young children at a small outdoor daycare area with swings, as a caregiver stands nearby, illustrating wartime childcare in a suburban context.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Housing design, Housing theory, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Multi-Plex Housing
Project Description:
Foregrounding the contemporary housing crisis as co-constitutive of the gendered and spatial crisis of caregiving, this paper advances a feminist critique of suburban housing and planning policies in Toronto. It centers Scarborough—one of Canada’s most underserved childcare districts—as a critical site of analysis, where the shortage of childcare infrastructure is compounded by ongoing suburban densification. This transformation is driven by mid-rise development and the proliferation of additional dwelling units under the City’s Expanding Housing Options in Neighborhoods (EHON) initiative, which currently proceeds without an adequate framework for the corresponding expansion of childcare capacity. In response, the project calls for the systematic integration of childcare infrastructure into urban policy and new housing, positioning it as essential social infrastructure that must be coordinated with housing planning, policy, and design.

Moving beyond its consideration within affordability, labor markets, and social policy, the project examines childcare as a problem structured through architecture and urban planning. It highlights the disconnect between contemporary densification policies and the region’s childcare infrastructure shortfall, while foregrounding how caregivers—primarily women, and especially women of color in low-income neighborhoods—negotiate the urban environment and perform invisible labor to sustain social reproduction. Through this analysis, the paper proposes amendments to EHON through the co-location of childcare infrastructure within new mid-rise housing along transit-rich Major Streets.

New triplexes, fourplexes, and sixplexes are introduced to increase density while maintaining the neighbourhood’s scale.
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Alexandra Park is a multicultural, lower-income neighbourhood in downtown Toronto shaped by repeated cycles of erasure and renewal. Originally developed as public housing following 1950s “slum clearance,” and again transformed in the late 2000s, the site reflects a tabula rasa approach to revitalization. Despite this, a strong and diverse community emerged, formalized through the 2003 conversion to the Atkinson Housing Co-operative—the first of its kind in Canada—granting residents collective control over their housing. 

Today, this community faces renewed pressure. Recent proposals by Tridel prioritize market-rate units over rent-geared-to-income (RGI) housing, threatening displacement. In response, SOCA proposed an alternate vision that retains the existing townhouses while relocating some residents to new condos.

Our proposal aims to retain as many townhouses and existing residents as possible in order to preserve the neighbourhood’s small-scale, socially cohesive character, while increasing density to accommodate Alexandra Park’s growing population. The main pedestrian-oriented pathway (Vanauley Walk) is central to the site; to activate it and improve safety, 50% of the existing ground-floor townhouses are converted into commercial or live/work spaces, allowing residents to operate businesses below their homes. Given the small unit sizes, two ground-floor units can be combined to create larger commercial spaces for uses such as a pharmacy, or reconfigured into accessible units. Newly proposed triplexes, fourplexes, and sixplexes help accommodate displaced residents within similar housing typologies. Although a larger community center is proposed along Grange Ave, smaller scale community spaces such as a shared kitchen, lounge and dedicated youth and senior drop-in areas are integrated along Vanauley Walk to extend programmatic access and support everyday social interaction at a more intimate, neighbourhood scale.

Project Title: The Ontario Science Centre: A Call for Evolution
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Intervention on the Ontario Science Centre Building B - Proposed Observatory/Look out.
Student Name(s): Nava Mahouri Monfared, Carolina Ramirez Tenorio
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Pedagogy, Agriculture and Gardening, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
A Call for Evolution stands as a manifesto against the privatization of the Ontario Science Centre. We advocate for a programming evolution that honors Raymond Moriyama’s original vision: a public cultural asset rooted in the nature of the site.
We firmly reject proposals for office-heavy privatization of the building. As a non-negotiable community anchor, the Science Centre is becoming one of Toronto’s most accessible nodes via the new Ontario Line and Eglinton LRT. Privatizing this landmark would strip the community of a vital resource just as it becomes most reachable. Our proposal follows Moriyama’s own directive that the building’s program be reassessed every 15 years, pivoting the OSC toward ecology and hands-on sustainability.
While the original design framed the Don Valley, it lacked a mechanism for visitors to physically engage with the ravine. Our design expands the architecture through branching stilted pathways and satellite buildings. This is informed by Moriyama’s childhood experience building treehouse while in a Japanese Canadian internment camp, an act of finding solace in nature that defined his lifelong architectural philosophy.
By using stilted substructures, we respect the delicate wetlands and watershed while allowing direct immersion into the Woodlands, Open Lands, and Meadows. Through new spaces for urban agriculture and outdoor classrooms, we transform the site into a living laboratory. This proposal aimes to ensure the Science Centre remains a public, accessible, and essential educational heart for all people.

We’ve mapped exactly where it is appropriate to build and where we must stay sensitive to the watershed. We’ve identified three distinct zones: the Meadows, Open Lands, and Woodlands. Each has unique flora, fauna, and soil conditions.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
“Brighter Waters” is a counterproposal for Port Credit that challenges prevailing models of heritage conservation and suburban growth. In contrast to the adjacent Brightwater development, critiqued for its siloed approach to activity, connectivity, and densification, the project proposes a more integrated urbanism that is active, continuous, and capable of evolving. Working across urban and architectural scales, it introduces new east–west connections that link the Heritage Conservation District to the town center, waterfront, and key transit nodes.

The proposal focuses on the Old Port Credit Village Heritage Conservation District, understood not only as a protected enclave but also as a spatial and regulatory barrier to broader connectivity. Rather than treating heritage as fixed, the project advances a model of targeted, incremental redevelopment that enables adaptation while maintaining material and cultural significance. Conservation is reframed as an active, ongoing process.

At the architectural scale, the project studies a representative property containing original brick from the historic Port Credit Brick Company, embedding local industrial history within its construction. The house is reconsidered as part of a larger assemblage with an adjacent property, forming a combined site with greater capacity for intensification, programmatic diversity, and spatial reorganization.

The proposal explores regenerative reuse through additions, mixed-use programming, shared space, and selective preservation. Mature trees are treated as organizing structures, extending designation beyond the building to the landscape. The house is activated through new uses and connections, allowing it to participate in neighbourhood life.

By aligning material continuity, incremental growth, and public connectivity, “Brighter Waters” strengthens relationships between past, present, and future urban conditions.

Project Description:
This project is a mixed-use civic and residential building located in downtown New Westminster, at the intersection of Columbia Street, Front Street Mews, and McKenzie Street. The proposal combines a public library with affordable housing to create a single, integrated building that supports both community life and long-term living.

The library forms the civic base of the project, acting as an accessible public resource that goes beyond traditional programming. It provides spaces for learning, gathering, and support services such as job assistance, language resources, and community outreach. The goal is to position the library as everyday infrastructure that serves a wide range of users, including newcomers and the broader public.

Above, the residential component follows a mixed housing model, combining non-market housing with long-term stabilization housing for newcomers. This approach supports both permanent residents and individuals transitioning into stable housing. Units are designed to be flexible, adaptable, and supportive of different household needs, prioritizing dignity, privacy, and long-term livability while avoiding institutional qualities.

A key idea in the project is the blending of public and residential users through shared spaces that encourage interaction while still maintaining clear boundaries. These spaces create opportunities for connection without forcing it, allowing residents and library users to coexist comfortably.

The building responds to its urban context by addressing the slope of McKenzie Street, engaging the more public Columbia Street edge, and activating the quieter Front Street Mews. The overall design aims to feel cohesive as one building, reinforcing the idea that housing and public life are interconnected.

Environmental systems are integrated throughout, including a green roof for rainwater collection, solar panels, natural ventilation, high-performance envelope, and a geothermal system below grade.

Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Towers, Mixed Use, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
A second, miniaturized version of Canada's Earth Tower, theorized and purposed by Perkins & Will with the same goals and vision: To be the livable, high-performance, mass timber residential tower of the future. 

The project achieves Passive House Classic levels of performance, while challenging the typical "vancouverist" window wall clad high-rise common around the lower mainland. 

This was done by breaking the monolithic tower form into three separate masses. Each mass is 7 to 8 storeys tall, a height similar to the 6-storey mid-rise buildings popping up all over the region. Which in my opinion, strikes a good balance between density, livability and socialability.

The horizontal breaks on the tower are shared outdoor amenity spaces, which allows residents access to the outdoors without having to go all the way to grade or up to the roof.

The library itself has carried this theme of the future into its planning and design. The library revolves two ASRS systems (like those used in IKB at UBC) which can retrieve books or items from storage racks for easy distribution and storage. Thus freeing up all the space that would've been taken up by stacks for more flexible/modern programming like maker spaces and study/group work areas.

Project Title: From Isolation to Connection: Reimagining Senior Housing as Collective Commons
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Sectional perspective showing senior-friendly circulation from residential buildings through a central atrium to the mall, with accessible paths and landscaped courtyards connecting buildings above ground.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description: This project explores how seniors’ social housing can move beyond isolated living to support everyday connection by retrofitting Toronto’s East York Town Centre and transforming a space built for consumption into one that supports care and community. Rather than separating housing and retail, the proposal reimagines the mall as shared social infrastructure, integrating daily services, informal encounters, and accessible public life. The design introduces shared programs, clear and inclusive circulation, and spaces that encourage interaction while still allowing privacy, balancing independence with collective living. An indoor connection between the residences and the mall enables safe movement during winter, while light-filled openings improve spatial quality in lower levels. Responding to the Right to Housing, the project strengthens habitability, accessibility, and a sense of belonging, proposing housing not only as shelter but as a framework for social life, intergenerational exchange, and mutual support.

Project Description: Tower-with-its-Park reimagines Toronto’s St James Town’s postwar tower-in-the-park housing as community-centered, dignified homes. Once designed for the working class, the privately-owned towers now house lower-income immigrant communities in overcrowded, neglected conditions typical for the aging, inequitable rental stock. This thesis demands for sustained reinvestment in Toronto’s housing towers to ensure habitable living conditions, support community life and cultural exchange. Deploying a speculative public-private retrofit funding model, this project upgrades infrastructure, integrates environmental strategies, and reconfigures units for diverse households with flexible partitions and expanded balcony enclosures, while reclaiming shared grounds through vibrant ground-floor communal programs and diverse green space.

Project Description: Many unhoused residents in Toronto are repeatedly displaced across shelters, encampments, and informal urban spaces, without stable access to services. This project focuses on 35 Bellevue Avenue in Kensington Market, a publicly owned site within one of the city’s most service-dense areas. Image Description/Alt Text\n: An oblique site plan and perspective section of the repurposed abattoir.
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Cooperative housing, Financialization, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Mixed Use, Agriculture and Gardening, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
In the 1960, the City mistook the Civic Abattoir as a temporary financial liability for a permanent burden. By selling to Quality Meat Packers, the abattoir that was built to prevent monopolization was traded for a one-time payout, ultimately surrendering the once public land to exclusive private development. The counterproposal begins in 1960 — the City retains the site of the abattoir rather than surrendering it to private ownership. Along with the adjacent Wellington Destructor and city-owned land, the abattoir becomes an opportunity for community-focused public development. 

The buried Garrison Creek will be retraced conceptually. 2 Tecumseth Street will transition from industrial slaughter to a city-owned living commons of affordability, creativity, and resilience.

In the era of deindustrialization and reintroduction of the creek we propose: 
Affordable housing, including co-ops and RGI units,
Creative workshops and artist markets to replace industries, 
Resilient communities supported by the Creek and public amenities. 

The main abattoir building is repurposed for a municipal-run grocery store, community kitchens,  and creative studio spaces. The facade is modified to reflect the program change and the skylight is extended over the proposed interior street.

Project Title: Rooted Reuse: Reclaiming Industrial Heritage for Collective Artist Housing and Production
Image Description/Alt Text\n: An interior perspective sketch of a flexible circulation space doubling as a shared space.
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Community land trusts, Cooperative housing, Financialization, Housing design, Housing theory, Housing Production, Participatory Design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description: Adaptive reuse of light industrial buildings can address Toronto’s housing crisis while preserving cultural heritage. This thesis explores how reclaiming these disused structures can support live/work artist communities and advance the right to housing through affordability, security of tenure, and accessibility. The project calls for the transfer of underused industrial building stock into the stewardship of a cultural land trust as a city-wide network of permanently affordable artist housing and workspaces. Rooted Reuse treats existing structures as durable shells, inserting maker spaces within and layering housing above to form a dense, multi-level neighbourhood ecosystem that sustains livelihoods and cultural continuity. Developed through a prototype proposal at 40 Wabash Avenue, the project tests a replicable model for broader city-wide implementation.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Left: Site plan at 1:500 scale depicting the site and scale of the project. Right: The ground floor plan that houses an auditorium, backstage areas, administrative rooms, and multiple makerspaces.
Image 1 Caption: The project anchors itself within an existing industrial footprint, retaining the industrial shell as a spatial and cultural datum. At grade, the building operates as a public-facing plinth: a large auditorium, administrative functions, and distributed makerspaces establish a porous civic interface that supports both production and gathering.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Second and third floor plans showing a dense arrangement of makerspaces organized around circulation corridors and a central commons. Insets depict a shared workshop with long tables and tools, and a flexible exhibition space with wall-mounted artwork and open floor area.
Image 2 Caption: Upper plinth levels intensify productive use through a range of makerspace scales organized around shared work commons. Flexible exhibition areas are embedded within circulation, allowing production and display to coexist, reinforcing the building as an active cultural infrastructure rather than a static container.

Project Description:
“For nearly every injustice in the world, there is an architecture that has been planned and designed to perpetuate it.” The words of architect-activist Bryan Lee Jr find no greater truth in Canada than in the housing of migrant farmworkers who come under the Temporary Foreign Workers Program, which has been described by the UN as a “breeding ground for contemporary forms of slavery.” Decades of research and advocacy have unequivocally shown that their housing conditions are substandard and undignified.

In response to the Canadian government’s failure to address this issue, a coalition of academics, public health experts and advocates proposed the National Housing Standards in May 2025. Despite their critical role in housing design, architects were not part of this crucial dialogue — underscoring the disconnect between human rights advocacy and architectural research and design. This thesis project aims to bridge this gap by demonstrating that architects can contribute to migrant justice as historians of the built environment, advocates for housing justice, and visionaries of alternative futures. 

It lays the groundwork for a larger study that traces the genealogy of persistent dehumanizing housing practices from slavery to indenture, to contemporary parallels of both in Canada.

It integrates human rights reporting with architectural visualization techniques to reconstruct a migrant farmworker bunkhouse through the testimony of a former occupant, highlighting violations of various aspects of migrant workers’ Right to Adequate Housing.

Lastly, the project translates the proposed National Housing Standards into practice by reimagining an existing community of 550 farmworkers in Leamington, Ontario, in accordance with them. It also provides key recommendations for further strengthening the proposed standards, laying the foundation for a crucial dialogue between architects and advocates to collectively demand dignified housing for migrant workers across Canada.

Project Description: By retrofitting the housing and residential blocks of the Toronto suburb of Scarborough-Agincourt, Thresholds of Care demands suburbs that support the needs of diverse seniors aging in place.  The homogenous built form of Agincourt does not reflect the diversity of its primarily Chinese- and South Asian-Canadian residents, who are increasingly aging and who often care for elders by living together multigenerationally. Thresholds of Care adds space for multi-generational households through housing additions and ADUs. By deploying culturally specific uses of spaces such as sunrooms, wet kitchens, and gardens, along with parkettes, mid-block paths, spaces to rest, and mixed uses at block corners, the thesis brings daily life into the residential block, creating a more accessible, liveable neighbourhood for everyone.

Project Title: A Place to Stay: Reparative Architecture for Black Cultural Continuity in Little Jamaica
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Axonometric view
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Community land trusts, Decolonization, Housing design, Housing theory, Housing Production, Participatory Design, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Laneway Housing, Multi-Plex Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Toronto’s Little Jamaica along Eglinton West Avenue has long been a vibrant cultural hub for the Afro-Caribbean and Jamaican Black community. However, commercial avenue densification plans and a decade of LRT construction have contributed to a 13% decline in the area’s Black population. This pressure is further intensified by speculative housing developments by corporate builders, which raise commercial and residential property values and rents.

This thesis calls for reparative architecture through permanently affordable housing, commercial spaces, and cultural amenities owned by Black-led community land trusts. The project reimagines mixed-use housing along Eglinton Avenue West and laneway residential multiplexes along Reggae Lane. It proposes lot-by-lot densification of the area’s narrow lots to retain its fine-grain architectural character.

Ground floors of the mixed-use buildings are designed for precarious Black businesses identified by the community. Along Eglinton Avenue, these include a restaurant and hair-braiding salon. The Reggae Lane multiplex includes a small cultural café, while the Lanark Avenue building offers a quieter live-work space, such as a home salon within a two-floor unit.

Housing types respond to residents’ cultural needs. Alongside the 1- and 2-bedroom units most needed by the community, the project includes multigenerational and intergenerational units. The idea of Black home extends beyond private dwelling into porches and courtyards as gathering spaces. Since informal gathering is central to the area, flexible shared spaces are integrated throughout. Balconies function as vertical porches, and circulation corridors create porous connections from Eglinton through Reggae to Lanark Avenue while supporting informal meetings among neighbors on every floor. The project is conceived as a speculative Community Land Trust proposal to ensure Black residents can live, work, gather, and sustain their identities without fear of displacement.

Project Description: The neighbourhood surrounding Allen Gardens has been a long-standing affordable neighbourhood for a variety of inhabitants. Throughout the last seventy-five years the heritage mansions that composed this area have transformed into apartments that are sub-leased for rooming. Like many central neighbourhoods, it is experiencing gentrification with the recent influx of high density towers. Supported by CMHC, this mid-block midrise project on the east side of Allen Gardens transforms an existing parking lot into desperately needed affordable housing. The project supports essential hospital workers and their families who have been priced out of the downtown core. The Courtyard Collective gently integrates between a small outpatient hospital to the north and a midrise residential apartment building to the south.  It provides ground related two-storey town houses on the east side of the property for families. This strategy provides multiple frontages to the housing, further animating the generous laneway behind. Central Hospital Lane features emerging laneway housing which unites the project to the existing neighbourhood. Residents have access to private and public terraces and community focused facilities that encourage gardening for health and self sufficiency. All programs and units are oriented towards the central courtyard which cultivates a secure environment for the residents and public.

Project Description:
The project explores a mixed-use architectural approach that integrates a public library with multi-generational shared housing to create a socially connected urban environment. Organized through a layered system of public, semi-public, and private spaces, the design emphasizes interaction, accessibility, and community living while maintaining clear spatial boundaries between programs.

The residential component is designed using a point access block strategy, allowing each floor to maintain efficient circulation, increased daylight access, cross ventilation, and stronger community connections through shared courtyards and outdoor terraces. The library podium acts as a civic anchor at the street level, activating the public realm through transparency, visual connectivity, and open gathering spaces. Above, residential volumes are arranged around interconnected communal spaces that encourage social interaction while preserving privacy for residents.

Exterior circulation bridges and vertical connections reinforce the concept of layered interaction by visually linking residents to shared spaces and public activity below. The architectural language combines warm brick cladding with large glazed surfaces to balance solidity and openness, while repetitive arched openings create rhythm, identity, and human scale across the façades. Roof gardens, terraces, and outdoor activity spaces further extend communal living beyond the interior, supporting well-being and collective experiences within the building.

Overall, the project proposes a hybrid civic-residential typology that promotes urban equitable livability by merging learning, gathering, and living into a cohesive architectural framework centered around community, connection, and shared experience.

Shared terraces, exposed circulation, and articulated brick façades emphasize community interaction, visual connectivity, and the relationship between public and private spaces.

The contrast between the solid upper volumes and the transparent glazed podium highlights the transition from active public spaces at street level to private living spaces above, creating a balanced urban presence along the streetscape.

Community gardens are distributed across the rooftop terraces at Levels 7, 8, and 9, utilizing the roofs of each residential volume to create layered green spaces for urban gardening, gathering, and outdoor activity throughout the building.

Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Towers, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Located in downtown Kitchener at 80 Young Street, adjacent to a school and within walking distance of City Hall and major transit connections, this project responds to a dense civic and educational context by asking how a street can be brought to life within an urban condition already defined by streets. It explores how residents and community can be meaningfully connected through flexible spatial systems that extend beyond the ground plane and into the building.

At ground level, an active civic base accommodates retail, an event hall, and shared community spaces organized around a central courtyard, forming a continuous interior streetscape for gathering, exchange, and everyday public life. Rather than a single exterior street, this courtyard becomes the core organizing space, extending the logic of the city inward.

A middle terrace between podium and tower establishes a second, elevated communal landscape for residents, creating another layer of streetscape with amenity spaces and informal gathering zones. Above, residential units are organized within a CLT modular system, allowing spaces to expand, contract, and adapt over time in response to changing needs.

The façade is conceived as a layered system of bio-based timber panels and colourful aluminum elements with integrated planters, supporting durability, environmental performance, and long-term adaptability. Permeability is expressed through varied balcony and façade conditions that respond to surrounding streets and interior courtyard edges, linking private and communal life.

Continuous skylights, generous shared spaces, and clear wayfinding strategies organize the building’s layered public, communal, and private realms, supporting both student living and long-term residency. The project establishes a flexible framework where courtyard and circulation spaces form a continuous, evolving civic and residential landscape, connecting daily life across multiple scales.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Located at 80 Young Street in downtown Kitchener, the site is surrounded by schools, churches, and community-oriented spaces, with nearby parking areas and convenient transit connections. However, rather than simply relying on these existing amenities, the project seeks to activate and extend community life directly within the site by transforming it into a layered, inhabited streetscape that supports gathering, connection, and everyday public engagement.
Image 2 Alt/Description: The project reinterprets the street as a vertical condition. Built to the site boundary, the mass is carved to form a central courtyard and opened along its edges to invite movement through the site. This courtyard, paired with a sunken event space, establishes a public core, while a series of terraces and shared balconies create a gradient of gathering spaces above. Varying heights and façade conditions shape moments of connection and privacy, forming an interconnected network of elevated streetscapes that extend community life beyond the ground plane.
Image 2 Caption: Axonometric
Image 3 Alt/Description: While the tower accommodates primarily one- and two-bedroom units, the podium enables more flexible living arrangements, including larger family units, co-housing, and student-oriented layouts. A modular prefabricated timber system supports long-term adaptability.  The project emphasizes generous shared amenities and varied programming, creating a network of spaces for residents and the public that encourage interaction, support daily life, and extend community beyond the unit.
Image 4 Alt/Description: With a school nearby, a residential neighbourhood to one side, a major transit path, and City Hall just below, the project responds by activating the ground plane as a lively public interface. Retail spaces line the sunnier edge of the site, anchored by a café at the corner, drawing in both daily commuters and the surrounding community. Existing on-site parking is retained to support the school and visitors to the retail spaces.  An upper pedestrian-focused path prioritizes active movement and community use, incorporating bike parking and a workshop, along with a playground that serves both local families and children from the adjacent daycare. At the center, a sunken courtyard creates a sheltered public space and is paired with a flexible event hall that can accommodate markets, gatherings, and community events.  Surrounding this core is a network of spill-out spaces that vary in scale and intimacy, supporting different modes of gathering, from informal social interaction to quieter retreat. A neighbourhood-oriented supermarket anchors the upper corner of the site, strengthening connections to the residential context across the street, while two distinct lobby entrances provide clear and accessible points of entry into the building.
Image 4 Caption: Ground Floor Plan
Image 5 Alt/Description: Within the modular framework, a range of rearrangeable spatial components is developed to support different living needs, along with an additional “block” that residents can customize based on their preferences. In this example, a three-bedroom unit is adapted by students sharing the space, configuring it to suit their routines and lifestyles.  Across the building, while the primary structural framework remains fixed, a system of flexible partitions and demising walls allows units to be reconfigured over time. This enables a high degree of customization, supporting a variety of households, from students to families, and allowing spaces to evolve with changing needs.
Image 6 Alt/Description: The structural framework is composed of CLT timber columns, beams, and floor panels, significantly reducing embodied carbon compared to conventional concrete construction. Organized on a 3 m and 6 m grid, the system establishes a consistent and efficient layout, while services are consolidated along the central corridor to maximize flexibility and adaptability within units.  At the podium level, prefabricated aluminum panels introduce a vibrant layer to the façade, adding colour and identity to the lower portion of the building. Above, the tower and amenity levels are clad in bio-based, light wood prefabricated panels, reinforcing the building’s material continuity and environmental performance.
Demands: Demand housing that evolve with people, adapt to climate, and foster collective life—

Project Description:
Recharge Commons is a mid‑rise residential community proposed for Southwest Kitchener on a site currently occupied by a church, single‑family home, event shed, and expansive green space backing onto a hydro corridor. The project introduces four mid‑rise buildings constructed in CLT with brick cladding, a biogenic material palette that echoes the surrounding neighbourhood while reducing embodied carbon. The buildings frame a series of intentional open spaces, including a reconstructed wetland ecosystem, to create a dense yet ecologically and socially resilient development.

Located within a key groundwater recharge zone feeding the provincially significant Strasburg Wetland Complex, the project prioritizes permeable land and vertical growth over sprawl. Bioswales along building edges direct stormwater toward a constructed wetland park, enhancing biodiversity, supporting the recharge cycle, and offering recreational trails. Massing strategies and passive shading devices shape comfortable microclimates between buildings, while stepbacks and planting buffers soften the transition to adjacent single‑family homes.

Programming is intentionally blended rather than zoned. Ground floors host co‑working areas, event spaces, a café, and communal laundry, while upper levels include community kitchens connected to rooftop terraces. Generous stair landings, visible circulation paths, and layered social spaces encourage spontaneous interaction. A square planning grid minimizes circulation within units and creates flexible, well‑proportioned living spaces that support resident agency. Units are strategically organized to create opportunities to merge single-bedroom and two-bedroom units for larger living arrangements. The landscape is equally diverse, with seniors’ gardens, children’s play areas, sports fields, community gardens, and the wetland trail network extending into the hydro corridor.
Image 1 Alt/Description: A view from one of the recessed balconies to the flexible sports field, which is lined with metal walkways and bioswales. There are opportunities for artists who live in the buildings to propose and paint murals on the lower two levels of parking garage walls that face the communal green spaces.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Four buildings are proposed on this Southeast Kitchener site. A ten-storey high-rise fronts the roundabout intersection, lowering to an eight-storey mid-rise building closer to the hydro corridor. Two bar-type mid-rises are nestled in the center of the site, the longer one standing at six storeys and the shorter one at four storeys. The two taller buildings block cold western winds while only casting shadows into the large intersection. The site planning makes use of massing and passive shading devices to shape distinct green spaces with comfortable microclimates.

Image 3 Alt/Description: At Recharge Commons, shared gathering spaces are not limited to the ground floor but are dispersed across all floors. With libraries, communal kitchens, and terraces located on certain floors above ground level, these social spaces are established near primary circulation paths, increasing opportunities for chance encounters and promoting neighbourly interactions.

Image 4 Alt/Description: The site is within a  Regional Recharge Zone at the centre of three wetlands: the Laurentian and Borden Wetlands, Williamsburg Wetlands, and the Huron Natural Area. It is within cycling and transit distance of daily amenities, community facilities, and retail. The site around the buildings includes a northern social plaza, playground, and flexible sports fields, supported by a network of bioswales that drain toward a central retention pond.

Project Title: Strange Neighbours Affordable Housing, Productive Space, and the Architecture of Coexistence
Image Description/Alt Text\n: Residential Building on top of a Warehouse
Home Studio\n: Architecture and the Right to Housing — University of Toronto
Tags: Community land trusts, Cooperative housing, Financialization, Housing design, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Co-Living, Bar Buildings, Mixed Use, Agriculture and Gardening, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
Strange Neighbours proposes a district-scale conditional mixed-use intensification of underperforming employment lands in Toronto. The project argues that residential permission on protected employment land should only be granted when three conditions are met: productive capacity is retained or increased, land value uplift is permanently directed into affordability through governance rather than speculation, and architectural compatibility between industrial and residential uses is demonstrated through design.
The proposal tests this conditional bargain across three city blocks, each organized around a distinct productive ecosystem: infrastructure (data centre and district energy), food (food bank, commercial kitchens, and vertically integrated grow floors), and trades (fabrication workshops and assembly). Housing is stacked above and woven into each productive program, not simply placed on top of it.
Governance is structural. A Community Land Trust holds the land through a ground lease, enforcing productive GFA minimums and affordability covenants in perpetuity. A housing co-operative holds the buildings, with units covenanted at rents tied to median renter income. This structure is the mechanism that converts architectural coexistence into lasting public benefit.
The project does not argue that employment lands should be opened to housing generally. It argues that a credible bargain, enforced through governance and demonstrated through architecture, is the only condition under which that permission is earned.

Project Description: Kitchener’s Auditorium neighbourhood is key in the city’s rapid densification. Urban growth within the area is imminent due to its proximity to the downtown core and several major transit routes, yet is limited by its suburbanized and car-centric planning as well as lack of amenities. Growing Block is affordable, missing middle housing that seeks to shift the Auditorium neighbourhood into an active urban community and welcome Kitchener’s evolving demographics, creating healthy and adaptable living. The activated ground floor is lined with locally owned retail, community spaces and mutual aid centres, becoming a walkable core for the surrounding residents. A public face is created along the adjacent Knollwood Park and landscaped courtyards and corridors also offer semi-public space with a playground and community garden. The site features significant bike and pedestrian infrastructure to encourage residents to take advantage of their downtown proximity and limit their car usage while also bringing traffic to the site. Ontario’s population demographics are shifting with densification, with demands for housing beyond the traditional, single family home that occupies over half of the province’s residential infrastructure. These demographics include multigenerational immigrant families, singles, and households seeking mutual aid. Three building typologies were established: Work-live studios, multigenerational commons, and clustered co-living. Each building typology contains units and facilities that cater to housing needs while offering a high amount of adaptability within a changing city. Removable partition walls and shared amenities allow for resident agency as a solution to the provincial surge of over and under-housing. Permeable thresholds between semi-public and private are established including single-loaded communal balcony corridors, roof terraces, winter gardens and unit clusters to encourage residents to interact with each other at their own pace.

Project Description:
Located at the corner of Sherbourne Streets and Gerrard,Toronto, and adjacent to Doctor O Lane, this mixed-use residential project responds to the character of the surrounding historic neighborhood and its adjacency
to Allan Gardens. 

The area is known for its visible street art and graffiti culture, which inspired the project’s focus on creativity and expression. Rather than treating graffiti as something temporary or informal, the building embraces the artistic identity of the neighborhood and creates a place where different forms of art can exist, evolve, and be shared with the community. The building is designed primarily for artists, students, and families, with a focus on fostering a strong sense of community. 

Most of the residential units are three-bedroom apartments, encouraging shared living and collaboration between residents. At the center of the project is a large courtyard that organizes the building and becomes a central gathering space. Glass-lined corridors overlook this courtyard, creating visual connections between floors while bringing light and activity into the heart of the building. Throughout the structure, commercial artisan spaces are scattered among the residential levels, blending living and creative workspaces. Communal areas include an artist market, pottery studio, gallery, community workshop, individual fabrication rooms, and a digital fabrication lab. Protruding volumes extend from the residential units into the courtyard, forming semi-private spaces that act as extensions of the apartments and appear across every floor. Together, these elements create a building that reflects the artistic energy of the neighborhood while providing spaces where creativity, community, and everyday life can intersect.

Project Description:
The Two-Block Duplex on Central Hospital Lane in Allan Gardens Toronto, reimagines a tight urban laneway as an opportunity rather than a constraint. Located on the site of a Victorian house on Seaton Street in between Gerrard Street East and Carlton Streets, the project introduces housing for two additional households within an eight-meter-wide lot, accommodating two one-bedroom apartments with integrated working dens, all above grade.

The project is composed of two offset volumes, shifted vertically and horizontally to carve out distinct spatial moments within a compact footprint. Helical steel screw piles minimize site disturbance and preserve an existing mature tree. These shifts create layered setbacks that open sightlines and establish two contrasting courtyards: a semi-public front courtyard engaging the laneway, and a more private rear communal green space for residents. Each unit benefits from natural light and cross-ventilation from three orientations.

Beneath the upper volume, the offset form creates a sheltered pocket for a small commercial café, activating the ground plane. The massing steps down at the rear to two storeys to reduce impact on the yard, while each unit includes a private roof deck.

Rather than retreating into privacy, the duplex embraces its context. Through its articulated massing and integrated commercial program, the building fosters social interaction and adds character to the laneway, transforming it into a more communal and vibrant urban space.

Project Description:
This six storey assisted housing project is designed around everyday community life at the south east corner of Allan Gardens in Toronto. In the midst of this housing crisis, this assisted housing project is designed to meet the diverse needs of the local community. This site is central and well connected to transit, essential cultural and civic infrastructure and amenities such as hospitals and schools, and is at the doorstep of Toronto Metropolitan University. 

A mix of one to three bedroom units supports intergenerational living, bringing seniors, families and essential workers into close connection. The ground floor helps animate the street with shared programs, while a sunken courtyard at the corner creates a sunny space for gathering and local events. As the building steps back, each unit gains access to its own outdoor garden, which extends to a shared rooftop with planting areas and common amenities. The project focuses on reconnecting the building to the park, as well as on affordability, social interaction and community access to green spaces.

Project Description: Within a tight laneway lot of a lower income neighbourhood looking for change, Lamina Duplex proposes a lifestyle of vertical programming, commercial frontages, and a sense of both privacy and openness. Located on Woodward Evans Ln, the community is currently not in its best shape, but is moving towards change, with many murals put up in the last five years. On the colourful and narrow laneway, Lamina Duplex brings a bright commercial frontage, creating a reason for people to pass by the murals, and programmatically turning the laneway into a pedestrianized path. Entering through the shared yard, the residential first floor features two bicycle racks per unit, a small porch seat, toilet, washing machine, and a walk-in closet, along with storage underneath the stairs. The second floor features the main bathroom along with two bedrooms, one with a built in bed and desk with storage, and the third floor opens up to a large high ceiling living, dining and kitchen space. Through this narrow but long layout, stacked plumbing, through ventilation, and a greater sense of spaciousness is achieved. Clerestory windows are featured on the upper two floors of the house, which protect the residents privacy on the laneway side, while still providing lighting and ventilation. On the laneway, perforated steel screens are installed as a form of privacy, enhanced south light intake, and a form of public art. On the ground level, screens are installed as a bifold lift up door, providing an overhang when open, and greater security when closed. In a rapidly growing city, Lamina Duplex aims to increase density while further pushing the change the community seeks, as a precedent for what a new building in the community could achieve.

Project Description:
Situated within the historic Victorian fabric of Toronto’s Allan Gardens, a compact parking lot is transformed into a high-density, two-unit laneway house. Located near several vital community services, the design seeks to intensify underutilized land to provide housing for communities increasingly displaced by urban gentrification
The architecture serves as a strategy to challenge the existing frameworks of zoning, and planning strategies by establishing laneway homes as primary residential frontages that can be applied to sites all across the city. The concept rejects the typical stacked duplex, most commonly used in city contexts, by instead introducing an intertwined massing logic, where the building weaves and rotates two units together to solve the spatial limitations of narrow infill sites. This interlocking ensures that multiple orientations are a shared right rather than a luxury allowing all residents to gain equitable access to natural light, views and cross-ventilation which can often be elements sacrificed in dense urban dwellings.
As a repeatable prototype, the project replaces a suboptimal parking lot with a rhythmic geometry scheme that respects the scale of the neighborhood while pushing for a new mode of urban tenure. By prioritizing interesting geometry over optimized footprint, the project creates an engaging, sustainable living condition that proves densification can be a bright and compelling human experience that makes housing more than just a living environment.

Project Description: As one of the few major parks in Toronto’s downtown core, Allan Gardens is a significant gathering space for many people both from the neighborhood and for people from outside. However, the surrounding land is vastly underdeveloped, leaving the park as just a park, without holding the ability to fully incorporate its existence into peoples daily lives. Parklife aims to further enrich the area of Allan Gardens as a place of gathering, while providing residence for people looking to live a life next to a park. Solving an issue of the current site, the first floor of Parklife features a food hall with outdoor seating, replacing the dark alleyway currently in its place, bringing a place of gathering for people of the community, and food access for people looking to spend time at Allan Gardens. Parklife also features a supermarket, a laundromat, and a childcare centre, providing significant social convenience and support for those in the community. For people living in the adjacent tower in the same complex, the passage is provided through a far livelier food hall, which then opens up to a community courtyard for residents to gather within their complex as well. The residential units of Parklife features many two storey units aimed for family living, a demographic which would highly benefit from park adjacency, along with the public amenities provided by the building. Units are joined with a glazed single loaded corridor on the north side facing the courtyard, providing a sense of social connection for residents of the complex. Replacing the traditional balcony which is unusable for majority of the year, Parklife features balconies with a modular screen with operable bifold coverings, providing a sense of enclosure while extending the usable period of the balcony. With through ventilation and daylight from both the north and south sides, residents of Parklife enjoy many features of a single family home, while still bringing the density appropriate for such an urban site.

Project Description: Growing Together is a housing co-operative that reimagines urban density by integrating community-focused typologies into a cohesive social and ecological framework. Two residential buildings frame a central community garden, creating a direct relationship between housing, landscape, and public life. Built on a site that originally included the Kitchener-Waterloo Seventh-day Adventist Church and a small community garden, the project transforms this space into a vibrant hub for the surrounding suburb. Through sustainable strategies such as natural ventilation, passive design, urban agriculture, and an optimized central atrium, Growing Together fosters an environment where living, nature, and community coexist. By dissolving boundaries between public and private space, as well as built and natural systems, the project proposes a progressive model for the future of co-operative housing and community-centered urban living.

Project Description:
Work/Eat/Play presents an urban densification strategy for J. Steckle Heritage Farm, a non-profit demonstration farm located on the outskirts of Kitchener, ON. This proposal bridges the non-profit’s mission to educate people on food production with an affordable housing model rooted in food sovereignty and security for its tenants, a continuation of Jean Steckle’s work with the United Nations and Canada’s northern Indigenous communities. Alongside an addition of over 200 residential units, Work/Eat/Play leverages the site’s uneven topography to introduce new agricultural microclimates and key infrastructure—such as classrooms, crop processing and storage areas, and a generous commercial kitchen—that will grow J. Steckle Farm’s public programming. These functions also support economic opportunities for the proposed community, including a central farmer’s market connected to a conditioned venue space that overlooks the existing heritage barn structure, supplementing its limited seasonal income from hosting weddings and conferences.  

The site’s poor transit access and surrounding pedestrian infrastructure informed a target demographic that is predominantly comprised of long-term residents that work remotely or are employed on the farm, as well as young urbanites seeking an agrarian lifestyle transition but currently lacking the required skills in self-sufficiency. Architecturally, this manifests in urban building typologies and densities that adopt intimate community sizes, an agrarian architectural language at the pedestrian scale, and individual access to green space. Demographic needs equally drive the design of individual units; Work/Eat/Play features highly appropriable co-housing, intergenerational, and live-work units, each designed to foster interpersonal relationships through the collective growing, cooking, eating, and sharing of food among residents.

Project Description: Canopy integrates circulation, collective living, shared space and cooperative housing into the urban fabric of downtown Toronto. Located between Allan Gardens and Central Hospital Lane, this midrise acts as a connector bridging a high activity landscape with a quiet and collected residential edge. Defined by subtraction and elevation, a lifted residential mass forms a large cantilever canopy contrasting the sunken courtyard creating a circulatory and inhabitable ground. The space supports movement, gathering and spaces for playing, resting and socializing. Ground level programming prioritizes permeability through glazing and public engagement seen through cafes, markets, and shared domestic spaces. Above, residential units take on a denser exterior organized around a central core for vertical circulation and lightwell atriums to encourage and ensure daylight, ventilation and visual connection. The transition of materiality through a heavy concrete upper mass to a lighter transparent base shows a transition from private dwelling to collective urban life, further supported by wood and plant accents enhancing life and outdoor space. Overall, this building acts as an urban connector through its use of outdoor space and circulation in a hardscape urban plaza.

Project Description: Located behind a recently renovated Conestoga College residence in downtown Kitchener, Collective 22 activates an existing parking lot along Queen Street North to provide high density co-operative housing. The building’s massing mediates the scale of the city by adopting the three-storey datum of the adjacent streetscape. The architectural form is defined by a deep setback above the podium, creating expansive, shared terraces that provide a balance of solar exposure and shaded relief. Below, a sunken courtyard establishes a highly sociable ground plane, integrating a public café and landscaped gathering spaces that prioritize human connection and the pedestrian experience
