Project Description: This midrise housing proposal at Sherbourne and Gerrard combines mixed-income residential units with shared amenities, daycare, retail, and a central courtyard to create a socially active urban block. The building includes a range of unit types, from studios to three-bedroom units, to support different household sizes and life stages. Ground-related program helps connect the project to the street, while the courtyard provides daylight, air, and a shared outdoor space for residents. The design also considers prefabrication and off-site component construction as a way to improve construction speed, reduce disruption, and support more efficient housing delivery. The project aims to balance density, livability, and community by combining private homes with collective spaces that encourage everyday interaction and long-term use.

Project Description:
Spence Neighbourhood Association’s mission is to provide housing and other support services to the residents within the Spence neighbourhood in Winnipeg’s West End, especially for the large Indigenous population that resides in the area. Currently, they wish to build an affordable housing complex within this neighbourhood that also has a variety of wraparound supports included. They want the building to be a place where the community can gather for events and activities, and where they can feel safe and supported (whether they live in the building or not). This project surrounds a design proposal for such a building.

This year was divided into two main parts: a material study, and a design proposal of a supportive housing complex, as envisioned by SNA. The challenge of this studio was not only to design a building with the outlined program, but to also intertwine our material studies within. The material study and housing projects were never meant to be two separate ideas; the focus of the project was to implement our material in a meaningful way within the context of the larger building project. 

My chosen material was fabric, and is applied throughout the building in multiple ways. The project began by using fabric formed into panels and placed on the walls as an object. Throughout the design, however, the material application evolved. The final project proposal uses fabric not only as an object on the walls, but also as an architectural element, used to shape the experience of the space throughout the building. Further, fabric becomes a programmatic element through the addition of a clothing exchange that not only supports the importance of textile recycling, but also connects the project to the larger community. Throughout the design, it was important to think not only about impactful ways to bring textiles into the project, but to always keep in mind the community of people the project strives to serve, and all while contributing to the making of “home”.

Project Description:
Concordia Commons plants itself as a new community node within Kitchener’s urban fabric. This co-operative housing project is built upon ideals of connection between residents and the larger community. By incorporating and redesigning the Concordia Club, Concordia Commons roots itself in existing traditions and community relations while combating housing alienation through an internal unit transfer style co-operative housing framework of adaptable, accessible unit types catered to a variety of individual and familial dynamics from families to students to couples.  

 

The orientation of streets and buildings on the site aims to create varying conditions for residents, ranging from large open field spaces, helping to fill a void of public park space within the surrounding area, to more intimate spaces such as the playground and gardens located at the core of the site. These differing spaces act in conjunction with the redesign and implementation of the new Concordia Club to cultivate places for activity, growth, and connection between both residents and the greater community.

Project Description:
This project explores how suburban housing can become more connected, flexible, and community driven. Located at 2150 Bleams Road in Kitchener, the site sits within a low density context where uses are separated and interaction is limited. The proposal introduces density while preserving qualities like light, openness, and privacy. 

At the centre of the design is an interior street that runs through the site, acting as both circulation and social space. Around it, a gradient shifts from public gardens and gathering areas to semi private terraces and private units. Affordability is approached through shared resources such as community kitchens, planting spaces, and flexible layouts. 

Five residential buildings form the core of the project. Stepping from five to eight storeys, they shape and frame the interior street, giving it definition and a strong sense of place. Their arrangement creates moments of openness and enclosure, turning the street into a lived environment rather than leftover space. These buildings also introduce adaptable units with non load bearing walls, along with shared winter gardens, rooftop terraces, and planted balconies that extend living beyond the unit. 

The north building acts as the public face of the project. With a café, retail spaces, and a food market, it activates the street edge and connects to the surrounding neighborhood through an open and welcoming design. 

In contrast, the south building is more inward focused and family oriented. It includes a daycare, gym, and shared spaces, with gardens and play areas that create a quieter, more intimate atmosphere. 

Together, the edge buildings connect outward while the central buildings frame a strong internal community organized around the shared street.

Project Description: Located at 315 East Ave in Kitchener, this project sits at a unique junction between the Kitchener Memorial Auditorium, a public park to the south, and a quiet residential neighborhood. The design splits the square site into three primary massings, using two diagonal corridors to pull the city’s energy through the heart of the block. This "X" circulation strategy carves out two specialized environments: a public-facing courtyard with commercial terraces that invite the community in, and a private resident courtyard featuring playgrounds and communal activities. By prioritizing interconnectivity, the proposal transforms affordable co-op housing for Union Cooperative into a vibrant social anchor that bridges the gap between its inhabitants and the surrounding urban fabric.

Project Description:
“As it Grows” is an affordable, community-oriented residential development located in downtown Kitchener, designed to support intergenerational living through adaptable and flexible unit design. The project emphasizes long-term usability, with residential units that can expand or contract in response to the evolving needs of families over time. Situated on a site that includes an existing three-storey adult learning centre, the building frames the street edge while opening inward to a large courtyard. This shared outdoor space is accessible to both residents and the public, fostering interaction and a strong sense of community between the two buildings and their users.

The ground floor is dedicated to residential amenity spaces, activating the courtyard by allowing interior programs to extend outward. These amenities include a daycare and playground, cafe, study areas, a senior lounge, communal kitchen, and lobbies all of which contribute to a vibrant and supportive living environment.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Field of Three is a cooperative housing project located on the Carmel New Church School site in southern Kitchener. The existing church and its elementary school remain an active part of the site, forming the foundation for a shared, intergenerational community where students, church members, and residents collaboratively shape daily life.   The project reimagines the village as a continuous field of overlapping spaces, where large and small environments flow into one another. It reflects what a village needs to thrive - places to gather, to rest, to play, and to think - brought together in a way that allows different rhythms of life to coexist naturally.   Community gardens, recreational areas, and quieter study and living spaces are interwoven, creating moments where activity and calm exist side by side. The school and church programs extend into the site, allowing children’s learning environments and community life to overlap meaningfully with housing. Field of Three creates a positive, shared landscape where generations meet through proximity, and everyday life is shaped by connection, care, and belonging.

Project Description:
This residential co-operative development explores the creation of a unified yet diverse home, both in its residents and spatial form. Drawing from missing middle and high-rise
typologies, the project challenges the categorization of these building types as separate, instead proposing a single block under a layered co-operative framework that provides the infrastructure for a thriving community. The townhouse, midrise, and high-rise components are flexible and adaptable, accommodating a wide range of residents’ needs and reconsidering how change can be designed for in housing.  
  At a more granular scale, the programming of the spaces and units responds to residents’ evolving needs and daily routines, creating variation in the mixed-use, amenity, and commercial spaces across the site based on the targeted users of the nearby residential units. The residential development specifically focuses on providing adaptable housing and the necessary resources to support multigenerational living, growing families, and aging-in-place demographics. The success of this cooperative development is rooted in understanding the wide variety of users’ needs and their dynamic, evolving nature.

Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Housing Production, Participatory Design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Towers, Row Housing, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description:
Located on 121 Westmount Rd E in Kitchener, Ontario, Westmount Place is an new mixed-market, urban redevelopment of a 1960s church site. Two axes “streets” are informed and framed by the existing church, three new residential buildings, collectively housing 69 new units (121 bedrooms), and a new daycare. The orientation of the new buildings responds to various site conditions: property lines, existing buildings, surrounding contexts, and parking requirements, which were all early negotiators. These two streets lead towards a central, elevated plaza, open for events and gatherings.

 Flanked on both sides by elementary and secondary schools, Westmount Place offers amenities for Montessori education, children’s music programs, Sunday school and Mass, along with additional community gardening, an open gymnasium, light groceries, and food and drink. Half-storey underground parking is located under the plaza, reducing the carbon footprint of the project. On-grade townhouses propose accessible elder housing; larger family homes are located above, fostering intergenerational connections and relationships. The mid-rise, six-storey atrium building, and taller eightstorey tower provide units ranging from 1 to 4 bedrooms, intended for post-secondary students and small families. From the Plaza, the tower’s podium frames the second street, and the new daycare, leading to above-grade parking for non-residents, allowing Westmount Place to be accessed by all community members.

Image 1 Caption: Reducing vehicular traffic was a crucial requirement for Westmount place. A laneway street wraps around the site, and parking is concealed to the side.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Street view rendering of the site from Westmount Road.
Image 2 Caption: Street view rendering of the site from Westmount Road. a major intention of the project was to have different building typologies create a "stepping" feel from the street, while ensuring building heights to be as low as possible, respecting the surrounding neighbourhoud context.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Tower Floorplans. Left: 2nd Floor Gathering Space and Terrace. Right: Floors 3-8
Image 3 Caption: The tower was designed as a Point Access Block, maximizing unit space, and reducing dead corridors.  Single and double bedroom units were designed for the tower.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Atrium Building Floorplans, Floors 2-6
Image 4 Caption: The Atrium Building was designed with the most units in mind. Deviating from the urbanistic common practice of single and double-loaded corridors, the atrium building aims to maximize sunlight and porosity for the residents and the site. Single, double, and triple bedroom units are initially proposed on a strict grid, with the ability to appropriate and move grid “spaces” around as one’s familial situation fluctuates.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Atrium Building, proposed typical floorplans and grid.
Image 5 Caption: Being a maximum of six storeys, the atrium building was allowed to be constructed with as mass timber-light frame hybrid. This enables residents to alter the non-load bearing walls in their unit and grids to accommodate their needs.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Townhouse Floorplans. Starting from the top, floors 1,2,3.
Image 6 Caption: Five townhouses, each containing two units can be found closest to the main street edge of the site, before the plaza grade change caused by the semi-underground parking lot. The first floor is intended for full accessible, elder living, integrating intergenerational peoples on the site. The upper two floors are designed for larger families, likely with young children, with a total of four bedrooms. Both units have an indoor garage at the back of the unit, accessible from surrounding laneway street.

Home Studio\n: Build Now to End Housing Alienation in Waterloo Region — University of Waterloo
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Courtyard Housing, Bar Buildings, Row Housing, Infill Housing, Multi-Plex Housing
Project Description:
Located in Forest Hill, Kitchener, the 311 Fischer-Hallman site sits in a mature suburban neighbourhood, with neighbouring properties dating from the late 1950s to the 1980s, mostly consisting of bungalows, single family detached houses, with a smaller selection of semi-detached and townhouses. This site contains an existing church, St. George's of Forest Hill Anglican Church, which is situated on the main road (and only frontage road) along with the clergy's house. Despite its suburban placement, the area is walkable, only a short distance away from parks, the grocery store, schools, and community facilities.
 
The design of our project is largely influenced by the site's configuration and positioning within the bigger urban area. Activated by the pocketed and nested condition of the site, the objective is to create a small scale community within a large-scale suburban context. The protective nature of the site, along with the surrounding network of schools, has programmatically shaped our project to be centered around families, with a mix of unit sizes to accommodate smaller families and larger multi-generational families, along with three distinct "courtyards" and shared interior common spaces throughout the interconnected buildings with different experiential qualities and characteristics.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Site Plan
Image 1 Caption: The annotated site plan highlights the robust network of existing facilities supporting the local area, and proposes how the amenities and residential form of Pocket Courtyards can integrate into this network.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Site Axonometric
Image 2 Caption: The site axonometric reveals the proposed built form in context, highlighting the site's characteristics and the overall massing of Pocket Courtyards: three linear bars cutting across the site, strategically scaled and articulated to respond to the surrounding conditions.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Ground Floor Plan
Image 3 Caption: The ground floor plan details the project's circulation strategy, negotiating pedestrian and vehicular movement across the site. Shielding pedestrians from a perimeter service road, Pocket Courtyards' built form creates a dedicated pedestrian-only street that connects various exterior spaces together.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Ecological Section
Image 4 Caption: The ecological section cuts through the pedestrian street to reveal two of the three distinct courtyards. The first, a more urban courtyard paved and enclosed on three sides, is heavily influenced by its occupants. Positioned between the church and parking structure, it functions as a street yet still supports gathering. The second, an elevated garden above the parking structure is south-facing and warmest in winter months.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Bar 1 Floor Plans
Image 5 Caption: The floor plans and units in Bar 1 explore aging in place and the potential residential-to-residential transition from 2-bedroom units to 1-bedroom and 3-bedroom units. Single-loaded with balconies opposite the corridor, this layout ensures cross-ventilation and provides unprogrammed extra space for residents.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Bar 2 Floor Plans
Image 6 Caption: The floor plans and units in Bar 2 also explore aging in place and our proposed residential-to-residential transition through an expandable modular unit. Double-loaded without balconies, this bar presents communal atriums and quiet areas as extended living spaces for residents instead.
Image 7 Alt/Description: Bar 2 Longitudinal Section
Image 7 Caption: This section-elevation illustrates a longitudinal cut through Bar 2 and reveals the North-West elevation of Bar 1. In section, Bar 2 balances the efficiency of vertically stacked plumbing with spatial flexibility. It utilizes an 'expanding module' concept: while plumbing cores remain fixed in plan, the spaces between them can adapt and flex through the movement of demising walls.
Image 8 Alt/Description: Row Housing Floor Plans and Views
Image 8 Caption: These stacked duplexes, accessible from the pedestrian street, were introduced as low-scale and familiar versions of multi-generational housing. The intentional inclusion of porch and balcony spaces provides valuable unprogrammed extra space, mediating the transition between the private home and the active pedestrian street.
Image 9 Alt/Description: Annotated Massing Model
Image 9 Caption: This annotated massing model highlights the four distinct components of our project.
Demands: Demand housing that evolve with people, adapt to climate, and foster collective life—

Project Description:
Through and Through is a mid-rise development on the southeast corner Allan Gardens, replacing an underused three storey 1960’s office building. It acts as a catalyst for community revitalization in an underserved neighbourhood. The infill project integrates shared amenity spaces, a daycare within the existing historic house, a local food market for produce grown on-site or within Allan Gardens, and an inward-focused café that supports both residents and the surrounding community.

The housing mix responds to diverse urban needs: 49% studio units support nearby students, 23% one-bedroom and 11% two-bedroom units serve downtown workers, and 17% three-bedroom units provide family housing with generous terraces for outdoor living. Agriculturally focused terraces and balconies create opportunities for resident gardening and promote sustainable living.

Public and private outdoor spaces are woven throughout the mid-rise, while the building’s cladding and landscape strategies extend the presence of Allan Gardens beyond its perimeter. At grade, an angular ground floor form guides pedestrian movement around the corner and creates generous garden seating.

A structural “push and pull” massing strategy maximizes daylight to each unit and is echoed in the rhythm of window sizing and awnings. The building’s blocking geometry derives from proportions extracted from the historic house, with materiality reinforcing this compositional logic and ensuring a respectful yet contemporary architectural response.

Project Description:
The Midrise proposal for Sherbourne and Gerrard Street East corner in Toronto opposite Allan Gardens Park creates an environment that encourages community connection. This new six storey building brings much needed subsidized housing for 16 new families and 16 singles in the centre of the city close to transit, schools, and jobs.

The design divides the program into two smaller buildings connecting at the fifth floor with a double height bridge running East-West, providing a significant social space for residents. The two connected cantilevered balconies overlook varying areas; Allan Gardens to the West, and the mature treed backyards of the Victorian homes to the East. In addition, the brick-lined bridge and 6-metre high floor-to-ceiling glazing, gives the fifth floor an outdoor feel as the facade's masonry transitions into the interior pathway of the bridge. With gardening opportunities on this fifth floor, Allan Gardens nature becomes an extension, forming an engaging streetscape.

The podium at grade provides a variety of retail and community amenity spaces such as a yoga studio, gym, bookstore, craft workshop, cafe, and daycare. The building is set back, drawing the sidewalk into the building, which emphasizes the connections between the individuals at grade and on the bridge. 

Creating these spaces brings together the community, allowing the environment to harbour more connectivity to bring individuals together through the Bridging Connections Midrise.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Laneway Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
The Bridged Laneway design efficiently utilizes space to create a welcoming environment for the residents to engage with, inside and out. The 6 meter wide site is located at the intersection of two lanes in the 19th Century neighbourhood east of Allan Gardens Toronto providing ample access to daylight from two sides behind a Victorian row house on Seaton Street. This project tests the height and density of the current laneway bylaws by providing two separate units and a commercial space in a 3 storey building rather than the current one unit allowed in two storeys above grade. The project retains the 120 year old tree in the middle of the site which initiated the form of the building. 

The Bridged Laneway is designed to house two units through a careful consideration of spatial orientation, natural light, and programming. The building is bifurcated into two blocks connected at the center by an elevated pathway that, on the second floor, becomes a shared balcony space, and a sun hallway on the third floor. The bridging defines the entrance into a private courtyard at the south end of the site. The entrances into both units are on the inside of the shared courtyard pathway and allow a more peaceful recessed entry from the public lane. The units intertwine between the two volumes so each gets access to morning and afternoon sunlight. Unit 1 includes the entire basement, and part of the first and second floors, while unit 2 contains part of the first and second floors and all of floor 3. The large doorways into the house's courtyard and its entrances become a part of the building, meant to further unify and bridge the individuals occupying the two homes. Part of the ground level is allocated to an art studio to support creativity in the area evident in the alley way murals and graffiti.

The project is shown as a replicable typology adding greater life and density along this underused lane in the heart of the city providing much needed housing for youth and artists.

Home Studio\n: The Porous Dwelling: Reimagining Housing as the Intersection of Architecture, Ecology, and Culture — University of Calgary
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Prefabrication, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing
Project Description: Living in Layers proposes an affordable housing project in downtown Canmore that supports local working families and young residents with a livable home that fosters both collective living and community building in a shared environment. It aims to challenge isolating dwelling models by designing for opportunities of connections through shared resources, spatial organizations, and everyday interactions. Located in downtown Canmore, at an intersection node connected to nature, residential and commercial districts, the project showcases how living in layers of programs and typologies can create mixed-use housing in Canmore. Integrating elements that promote collecting living and community building enhances the everyday experiences of shared living. The heart of the project, where all users and residents are framed together, is the central courtyard. The courtyard not only acts as a social core but also preserves the existing site elements of a pathway and trees that are of inherent value. Collective living in this project is supported by the organization of unit massing that create a porous network of housing, circulation, and connections. The ground-floor program spaces intentionally address local residents needs, offering adaptable spaces while creating revenue opportunities that maintain affordability. The public spaces enable community building by bringing together diverse user groups within the project and activating the larger community. Responding to the larger current housing challenges, and specifically in Canmore, the project provides an alternative housing model that fosters environments where collective living can be affordable. Further, without compromising construction cost, efficiency, and quality, prefabricated cross-laminated timber systems are used as the exposed structure for the project. Living in Layers reframes affordable housing as an inherent social and spatial experience that is shaped by everyday life as much as it is built form.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Valley Lodge in Canmore, AB. A new collective way of living
Home Studio\n: The Porous Dwelling: Reimagining Housing as the Intersection of Architecture, Ecology, and Culture — University of Calgary
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Row Housing
Project Description:
While housing affordability is a growing concern across Canada, few municipalities face pressures as severe as those in Canmore. Much of the town’s housing stock consists of oversized single-family homes and expensive condominiums that remain unattainable for many residents. Beyond cost, this condition creates a broader sense of disconnection between residents, housing types, and the surrounding landscape. Valley Lodge responds to this condition through a modular mass timber housing framework that prioritizes affordability, adaptability, and social connection.

The project establishes connection at three scales: site, building, and unit. At the urban scale, Valley Lodge occupies a site positioned between a quiet residential neighbourhood and the Bow River pathway system. Rather than following the surrounding pattern of detached homes with private yards, the proposal introduces multiple smaller buildings that lightly occupy the site, preserving generous shared outdoor space and creating a porous public realm connecting neighbourhood and landscape.

At the building scale, two rows of three-storey CLT housing are organized around a central sunken street. Large openings, facing balconies, and elevated timber walkways encourage movement and interaction between buildings. The architecture avoids unnecessary formal complexity, instead creating flexible spaces that support everyday encounters.

At the unit scale, split-level arrangements generate shared landings and threshold spaces between homes. Inspired by the Baugruppe model, future residents collectively shape the programming of these shared areas, allowing the architecture to evolve according to community needs. Valley Lodge ultimately proposes a transferable framework for attainable housing centered on connection between people, housing, and landscape.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Convergence. An affordable living collective in Canmore, AB
Home Studio\n: The Porous Dwelling: Reimagining Housing as the Intersection of Architecture, Ecology, and Culture — University of Calgary
Tags: Cooperative housing, Financialization, Housing design, Housing Production, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Material Flows and Sustainability
Project Description: In many residential neighborhoods, the single-family home represents an ideal of privacy, stability, and ownership, yet the reality of contemporary living rarely fits this model. Individuals live alone, roommates share housing, families grow and change, and more people work from home than ever before. These diverse living arrangements challenge the assumption that one house must serve only one family. At the same time, the cost of purchasing a home has made the traditional individual household model increasingly unattainable. Ultimately, Convergence asks how much potential is hidden within the familiar image of a house by introducing multiple households into a structure that appears to be a single-family home. It tests how density, affordability, and community might coexist within existing residential neighborhoods. While rooted in the conditions of Canmore, the project operates as a prototype, suggesting a model that could be adapted to other neighborhoods facing similar pressures. The result is not simply a new housing type, but a reconsideration of domestic life itself: a convergence where private and collective life coexists in a flexible, evolving home.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Exterior Perspective
Home Studio\n: Living Cooperatively — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Cooperative housing
Project Description: This project addresses Toronto’s “missing middle” housing by introducing a range of apartment units, from studios to three-bedroom layouts. The design prioritizes shared spaces and everyday amenities, including a rooftop terrace, a covered terrace at the fourth floor, a gym, study areas, and a drop-off daycare. An existing Victorian house on the site is retained and adapted into the daycare, with direct access to an outdoor play area. The project balances density with livability, using shared amenities and adaptive reuse to support a more community-oriented housing model.
